meta.title: The Great Refactor: Client to Server Components
meta.excerpt: How we reduced bundle size by 35% and improved performance

props.text: When we first built the electrical services website with Next.js, every component was a client component. This was the path of least resistance - everything just worked with "use client" at the top. But as the application grew, so did the JavaScript bundle. Page loads slowed, Lighthouse scores dropped, and mobile users on slower connections had a noticeably degraded experience.

props.title: Why This Matters
props.body: Server components are rendered on the server and send only HTML to the browser. This means zero JavaScript for static content like navigation, footers, headings, and layout wrappers. For a content-heavy site, this can eliminate 30-50% of the client-side JavaScript.

props.text: A thorough audit revealed that 100% of our components were client-side, despite most of them being entirely static. The Navbar, Footer, Hero section, service descriptions - none of these needed client interactivity, yet they were all shipping JavaScript to the browser.

We conducted a component-by-component audit, classifying each as either truly interactive (needs event handlers, state, effects) or static (can be server-rendered). The goal was to push "use client" as far down the component tree as possible.

steps.title: Audit
steps.description: Classified all 24 components as interactive or static. Found that 18 of 24 were purely static.

steps.title: Remove Top-Level use client
steps.description: Removed 'use client' from page.tsx and layout.tsx, making them server components by default.

steps.title: Create Client Boundaries
steps.description: Wrapped only interactive parts (form inputs, mobile menu toggle) in dedicated client components.

steps.title: Extract Interactivity
steps.description: Split large components: static wrapper as server component, interactive island as client component.

steps.title: Verify & Measure
steps.description: Ran Lighthouse, checked hydration boundaries, validated no functionality was lost.

props.title: Quick Win
props.body: The biggest gains came from the simplest change: removing "use client" from layout.tsx and page.tsx. This single change converted the Navbar, Footer, and all layout wrappers to server components, saving approximately 120KB of JavaScript.

features.title: Default to Server
features.description: Start with server components everywhere. Only add 'use client' when you genuinely need interactivity.

features.title: Push Boundaries Down
features.description: Keep client boundaries as low in the tree as possible. Wrap the smallest interactive piece, not entire sections.

features.title: Audit Regularly
features.description: Components evolve. A previously interactive component may become static after a refactor. Audit quarterly.

features.title: Measure Impact
features.description: Use Lighthouse CI, bundle analyzers, and real-user monitoring to quantify every architectural change.

props.title: Key Takeaway
props.body: Push "use client" down the component tree. Default to server components, only add client interactivity where genuinely needed. In our case, this reduced the JavaScript bundle by 35% and improved First Contentful Paint by 39% - all without changing any user-facing functionality.
